summary: This brief post shares Cleveland Browns player Andrew Hawkins' powerful statement about justice following protests over Tamir Rice's death, using it as inspiration for direct action. O'Neil endorses Hawkins' call that seeking justice shouldn't require apology or be seen as offensive. She announces her own planned protest at Citigroup headquarters, connecting the broader justice movement to Elizabeth Warren's critique of Wall Street from the previous week. The post links racial justice concerns with financial accountability activism.
key_points: Andrew Hawkins articulated that justice means fair treatment and the innocent being found innocent, and calling for it shouldn't offend anyone
O'Neil connects racial justice protests (Tamir Rice) with financial accountability activism (Occupy Citibank)
The post serves as a call to action, encouraging readers to stand up for causes they believe in
Warren's speech criticizing Wall Street provides additional inspiration for the Citigroup protest
money_quotes: I was taught that justice is a right that every American should have.
A call for justice shouldn't offend or disrespect anybody. A call for justice shouldn't warrant an apology.
Stand up for them. Speak up for them. No matter what it is because that's what America's about and that's what this country was founded on.
key_insight: Demanding justice—whether for police violence victims or financial system accountability—is a fundamental American right that requires no apology.
